---
title: "Polyglot parity (Python ↔ Julia)"
---

cositos is a **binding-free** core: the same widget frontend is driven from any Jupyter
kernel language, and every backend port serializes widget state to the *same*
[widget-state document](specs.qmd#serialization). This page proves it — the
tabs below build the identical document from the Python reference and the
[Julia port](porting.md), and the page **fails to render** if the two ever diverge.

The guarantee is pinned by a shared golden fixture (`fixtures/widget-state.json`): both
implementations certify against it in their own test suites, so parity is a checked law,
not a coincidence.

```{python}
# | echo: false
# | output: false
import json
import subprocess
from pathlib import Path

from cositos import dump_document

# The widget both backends serialize: an anywidget counter with one state field.
ESM = 'export default { render({ model, el }) { el.textContent = model.get("value"); } }'

# Python reference.
py_doc = dump_document([("counter", {"_esm": ESM, "value": 42})])
py_json = json.dumps(py_doc, indent=2, sort_keys=True)

# Julia port: run examples/parity/dump.jl in its own project and canonicalize its JSON
# through Python so the two tabs are formatted byte-identically.
root = Path.cwd()
while not (root / "mise.toml").exists():
    root = root.parent
jl_out = subprocess.run(
    ["julia", "--project=examples/parity", "examples/parity/dump.jl"],
    cwd=root,
    capture_output=True,
    text=True,
    check=True,
).stdout
jl_doc = json.loads(jl_out)
jl_json = json.dumps(jl_doc, indent=2, sort_keys=True)

# The parity law: identical documents, or this render aborts.
assert jl_doc == py_doc, "Julia and Python produced different widget documents!"
```

::: {.panel-tabset}

## Python

```{python}
# | eval: false
from cositos import dump_document

ESM = 'export default { render({ model, el }) { el.textContent = model.get("value"); } }'
doc = dump_document([("counter", {"_esm": ESM, "value": 42})])
```

```{python}
# | echo: false
print(py_json)
```

## Julia

```julia
using Cositos, JSON

ESM = "export default { render({ model, el }) { el.textContent = model.get(\"value\"); } }"
doc = dump_document([("counter", Dict{String,Any}("_esm" => ESM, "value" => 42))])
```

```{python}
# | echo: false
print(jl_json)
```

:::

Both tabs above show the *same* JSON, produced by two independent implementations. The
Julia output is generated by actually running the Julia port at render time (via
`examples/parity/dump.jl`); the assertion in the setup cell guarantees the documents are
equal, so this page can never silently drift out of parity.

## Next steps

- [Porting guide](porting.md) — Steps 3–4 show how to certify *your* language's port
  against the same fixtures this parity proof uses.
- [Serialization spec](specs.qmd#serialization) — the normative contract behind the
  identical JSON.
